title: NOC 32121 – Medical Radiation Technologists | GTR Immigration
description: Medical Radiation Technologists under NOC 32121 are highly skilled diagnostic and therapeutic imaging professionals working across Canada. With increasing cancer treatment programs, advanced diagnostic imaging expansion, and an aging population, demand for this TEER 2 occupation remains steady nationwide. For internationally trained professionals, NOC 32121 offers strong eligibility under Express Entry and Provincial Nominee Programs, creating a structured pathway toward Canada PR.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category3 – Health occupationsTEER2 – College diploma or apprenticeship training of two or more yearsMajor Group32 – Technical occupations in healthSub-Major Group321 – Technical occupations in health (except practitioners of natural healing)Minor Group3212 – Medical technologists and techniciansNOC Code32121

duties: Operate X-ray, CT, fluoroscopic, and mammography equipment
Position patients and apply radiation safety measures
Monitor and care for patients during imaging
Operate gamma cameras and radiation detection equipment
Conduct diagnostic procedures using radioactive materials
Operate linear accelerators and radiation therapy equipment
Perform equipment safety and quality checks
employment_requirements: Completion of a two- to three-year approved college program in:Diagnostic radiographyNuclear medicine technologyRadiation therapyMagnetic resonance imaging
Bachelor of Health Sciences may be required for some roles
Graduate certificate required for MRI specialization
Certification by the Canadian Association of Medical Radiation Technologists is required in all provinces except Quebec and British Columbia.
salary_prospects: Annual earnings typically range from $48,200 to $88,800, depending on specialization and province.
job_outlook: Expansion of MRI and nuclear medicine services
Specializations in radiation therapy and MRI may offer stronger job prospects.
labour_market_demand: This slight demand surplus reflects stable employment prospects, especially in provinces such as Ontario, Alberta, British Columbia, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba.
Why Immigrate to Canada as a Medical Radiation Technologist?
Long-term demand in oncology and diagnostic imaging
Canada continues investing in imaging technology, cancer care programs, and early disease detection initiatives.
pathways: If you are a qualified Medical Radiation Technologist, NOC 32121 may provide a pathway toward permanent residence in Canada.
Contact GTR Immigration today to begin your structured pathway toward permanent residence in Canada under NOC 32121.
